Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 29 of 2021; Suit No 630 of 2020
Metadata.Coram: Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA and Tay Yong Kwang JCA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd

Facts.Text: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd is a ship charterer and ship-management company operating a fleet of vessels and was incorporated in Singapore.
Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd and Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd are sister companies.
Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd made a payment of US$15.02 million from its Bank of America account to a Deutsche Bank account in the joint names of Lim Oon Kuin and Lim Huey Ching.
Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd made a payment of US$4 million from its Bank of America account to a Maybank account in the name of Lim Chee Meng Evan.
The payments of US$15.02 million and US$4 million were made from accounts of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd to bank accounts related to members of the Lim family.
At the time of the payments in April 2020, the Lims were the sole directors of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
At the material time, Lim Oon Kuin and Lim Huey Ching were shareholders of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
Lim Chee Meng Evan and Lim Huey Ching approved the payments on both occasions.

As at 29 February 2020, significant intercompany receivables of about US$185,054,244 were owed by Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd to Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd, and these receivables were by far the single largest debt owed to Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
The collapse in oil prices between the end of 2019 and 31 March 2020 placed a strain on Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd's liquidity.
Bank lenders had withdrawn or reduced credit lines to Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd as a result of decisions to reduce exposure to or exit the commodity trading market.
Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd's financial statements did not reflect about US$800 million in future losses that it had sustained over time, as a result of instructions given by Lim Oon Kuin.
The COVID-19 pandemic severely disrupted the operations of both Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd and Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd, resulting in a significant decrease in demand for oil and bunkers.

Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd discharged cargo carried for Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd on Lim Oon Kuin's instructions and/or on the basis of letters of indemnity issued by Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd to Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd rather than to the holders of the bills of lading, while the holders remained unpaid.
Lim Oon Kuin had provided unsecured and interest-free loans to Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd over the years, amounting to approximately US$225 million as at 31 March 2020.
Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd had a practice of discharging cargoes on letters of indemnity from Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether payments made by Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd in April 2020 from its accounts to bank accounts related to its directors and shareholders should be reversed or compensated for, in light of the financial condition of the company at the time of the payments.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors of a company must manage the company's affairs in a manner that takes into account the company's financial position and preserves the company's assets for the benefit of creditors when the company is in serious financial difficulty or close to insolvency.
Party_Details.Application: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd made two payments in April 2020 totaling US$19.02 million from its Bank of America account to bank accounts in the names of its directors and shareholders. At the time of these transfers, the corporate group faced collapsed oil prices, withdrawn bank credit lines, massive intercompany debt, and severe contingent exposures related to cargo discharged without bills of lading. These circumstances provide the factual context for assessing whether the extraction of funds by the directors was a breach of their fiduciary duties to the distressed company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the payments were made when the company was insolvent or in a parlous financial position. The directors breached their fiduciary duties by extracting company funds for their personal benefit instead of preserving assets for the creditors. The court rejected defenses claiming the company was independently solvent, finding that contemporaneous financial records and the massive, unrecorded liabilities of the sister company rendered the subject company effectively insolvent. Consequently, the company was entitled to recover the transferred funds from the directors.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 29 of 2021; Suit No 630 of 2020
Metadata.Coram: Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA and Tay Yong Kwang JCA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lim Oon Kuin
Counsel: Davinder Singh s/o Amar Singh SC

Facts.Text: At the material time, Lim Oon Kuin was one of the sole directors of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
At the material time, Lim Oon Kuin was a shareholder of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
At the material time, Lim Oon Kuin was a director of Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd.
On 3 April 2020, a payment of US$15.02 million from Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd's Bank of America account was made to a Deutsche Bank account in the joint names of Lim Oon Kuin and Lim Huey Ching.
Lim Oon Kuin had supported Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd by providing unsecured and interest-free loans over the years, amounting to approximately US$225 million as at 31 March 2020.
Lim Oon Kuin had given instructions not to reflect approximately US$800 million in futures losses that Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd had sustained.

Cargo carried by Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd for Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd had been discharged on Lim Oon Kuin's instructions without presentation of bills of lading, relying instead on letters of indemnity.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches fiduciary duties by receiving millions in company funds just prior to the company's collapse, despite having previously provided unsecured loans to the company.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must act in the best interests of the company and its creditors when the company is insolvent or nearly insolvent, and are strictly prohibited from prioritizing their own interests or siphoning corporate assets to the detriment of creditors.
Party_Details.Application: As a director, Lim Oon Kuin authorized and received a US$15.02 million transfer from the company to a joint account. This occurred concurrently with the revelation of hundreds of millions in hidden losses and liabilities across the corporate group. His prior unsecured loans to the company did not alter the company's distressed financial status or relieve him of his fiduciary obligations at the time of the transfer.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the director breached his fiduciary duties by receiving corporate funds for personal benefit while the company was in a dire financial state. The court rejected attempts to retroactively reconstruct the company's balance sheet to show solvency, ruling that the director had full knowledge of the massive hidden liabilities and unrecorded losses across the interrelated corporate group. He was held liable to repay the extracted funds.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 29 of 2021; Suit No 630 of 2020
Metadata.Coram: Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA and Tay Yong Kwang JCA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lim Huey Ching
Counsel: Davinder Singh s/o Amar Singh SC

Facts.Text: At the material time, Lim Huey Ching was one of the sole directors of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
At the material time, Lim Huey Ching was a shareholder of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
On 3 April 2020, a payment of US$15.02 million from Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd's Bank of America account was made to a Deutsche Bank account held jointly in the names of Lim Oon Kuin and Lim Huey Ching.
Lim Chee Meng Evan and Lim Huey Ching approved the payments on both occasions.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches fiduciary duties by approving and receiving substantial company funds into a joint account shortly before the company's financial collapse.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors and shareholders who approve or receive company payments to related-party accounts when the company is in serious financial difficulty or close to insolvency violate their duties to preserve company assets for creditors.
Party_Details.Application: Lim Huey Ching acted as a director when she approved a US$15.02 million payment to a joint account she held, as well as a US$4 million payment to another director. These transfers occurred simultaneously with the company's severe liquidity crisis and subsequent restructuring due to massive group-wide liabilities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court concluded that the director breached her fiduciary duties by approving transfers of company assets to related parties and her own joint account while the company was functionally insolvent. The court ruled that the directors could not ignore the catastrophic financial reality of the corporate group to justify extracting cash. She was held liable to return the funds to the company.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 29 of 2021; Suit No 630 of 2020
Metadata.Coram: Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA and Tay Yong Kwang JCA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Lim Chee Meng Evan
Counsel: Davinder Singh s/o Amar Singh SC

Facts.Text: At the material time, Lim Chee Meng Evan was one of the sole directors of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
On 13 April 2020, a payment of US$4 million from Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd's Bank of America account was made to a Maybank account in the name of Lim Chee Meng Evan.
Lim Chee Meng Evan approved the US$15.02 million payment on 3 April 2020 and the US$4 million payment on 13 April 2020.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director is liable for breach of fiduciary duty for approving and receiving a multi-million dollar transfer from an insolvent company.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors who approve and receive company payments to related-party accounts when the company is in serious financial difficulty or close to insolvency breach their fiduciary obligations to the company and its creditors.
Party_Details.Application: Lim Chee Meng Evan approved and received a US$4 million transfer, while also approving a US$15.02 million transfer to other family members. At the time, the corporate group was facing unrecorded losses, massive liabilities, and withdrawn bank credit lines, making the extraction of funds highly detrimental to the company's creditors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the director breached his fiduciary duties by authorizing the extraction of corporate funds for his own benefit when he knew the company and its sister entities were facing catastrophic liabilities. The court rejected defenses asserting that the company was independently solvent, ruling that the group's intertwined finances made the insolvency apparent. He was held liable for the return of the funds.
